quick_train.headline: case_101_cigna_mednec
quick_train.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Cigna has completed its review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider and received at our office on March 9, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated March 11, 2026.

After review by our medical review department, we have determined that the requested service does not satisfy Cigna's Medical Necessity Guidelines and is considered experimental and investigational. After review, we are unable to approve the requested service.

This determination is based on Cigna Medical Coverage Policy (CPG) 0234, "Pharmacologic Therapies — Coverage Criteria," version 4.2016, last reviewed June 2016 and scheduled for periodic reassessment. Under the criteria in effect, the requested medication is classified as experimental and investigational because the published evidence reviewed under this policy does not establish it as evidence-based for the submitted indication. Accordingly, the service is not eligible for coverage at this time.

You or your provider may request a copy of the claim file and the specific clinical criteria applied to this determination at no charge by contacting Member Services at the number on your ID card. A peer-to-peer discussion with a Cigna medical director may be requested within the timeframe noted below.

If you disagree with this decision, you have the right to file an appeal within 180 days of the date of this letter. Appeals may be submitted in writing to Cigna National Appeals, P.O. Box 188011, Chattanooga, TN 37422, or by calling the number on your ID card. You may submit additional information for consideration with your appeal.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Medical Review Department
quick_train.clinical_context: The patient is a 61-year-old woman with chronic migraine without aura (G43.709), averaging 15 headache days per month over the past eight months, with a documented MIDAS score in the severe range. Her neurologist has trialed and optimized multiple preventive agents: topiramate titrated to 100 mg daily for three months, discontinued for disabling cognitive slowing and paresthesias; propranolol 80 mg daily for roughly ten weeks with inadequate response and symptomatic bradycardia; and three abortive triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan, and eletriptan), which address acute attacks but provide no prevention. The submitted prior authorization requests erenumab (Aimovig) 70 mg subcutaneously once monthly, a CGRP-receptor antagonist FDA-approved in May 2018 for migraine prevention and supported by Category 1 pivotal randomized trials (STRIVE and ARISE) and the 2021 American Headache Society consensus statement. The specialist's letter and an eight-month headache diary documenting attack frequency, disability, and prior medication failures were included in the submission. Erenumab is indicated specifically for patients who have failed standard oral preventives, which this patient has demonstrably done.

quick_train.headline: case_145_cigna_mednec
quick_train.denial_letter_text:
Dear Ms. Member,

Cigna has completed a medical necessity review of the request for an extended suppressive course of metronidazole (twice-weekly intravaginal gel) for recurrent bacterial vaginosis and has determined that it does not meet the plan's medical necessity criteria.

An extended suppressive metronidazole course is not considered evidence-based for recurrent bacterial vaginosis under the plan's current medical policy. The published criteria the plan applies do not support authorization of suppressive therapy for this indication, and the request therefore does not satisfy the plan's coverage criteria. This determination applies even though the initial month of the suppressive course has already been dispensed and started.

Before any reconsideration, the plan requires that your physician submit additional clinical documentation, including the dates and results of the prior treated episodes. This documentation must be received within 48 hours of the date of this notice. No extensions will be granted, and if the documentation is not received within that window the determination will stand.

If you disagree with this determination, you or your provider may file an internal appeal by contacting the number on your member identification card within the timeframe stated in your plan documents.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Utilization Management
quick_train.clinical_context: The patient is a 46-year-old woman with recurrent bacterial vaginosis, with four Amsel-confirmed episodes in the past twelve months despite completing a standard single course of oral metronidazole for each episode. Her gynecologist prescribed an extended suppressive regimen of twice-weekly intravaginal metronidazole gel, which CDC sexually transmitted infection treatment guidelines and randomized trials specifically endorse for recurrent bacterial vaginosis to prevent relapse, contrary to the denial's assertion that suppressive therapy is not evidence-based. The pharmacy had already dispensed, and the patient had already begun, the first month of the suppressive course at the time this retrospective denial was issued, and the denial does not state whether she is now responsible for paying for the dispensed medication. Her gynecologist submitted the dates of the four prior episodes and the record of the completed standard courses together with the request. The plan's demand that further documentation be returned within 48 hours leaves no realistic opportunity to assemble and submit those records.

quick_train.headline: case_184_cigna_mednec
quick_train.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We have reviewed the recent request for behavioral health services submitted on your behalf. Following review under the applicable Cigna Medical Necessity Guidelines and our Medical Coverage Policy, we have determined that the request does not meet our criteria for coverage, and it is denied as not medically necessary.

Our medical policy provides that the requested service is not considered evidence-based for the submitted indication, and the published clinical criteria do not support authorization of the requested service. In addition, our policy requires that a member first complete and document failure of the applicable course of step therapy before a service in this category is approved. The information available to us does not establish that these policy requirements have been satisfied.

This determination is based on our standard medical policy in effect on the date of review. You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the claim file and the specific clinical criteria used to make this decision. Your treating provider may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a Cigna medical director.

You have the right to appeal this decision. A written appeal may be submitted within 180 days of the date of this notice, and you may submit additional information in support of the request. Please refer to your plan documents for instructions on where to submit your appeal.

Sincerely,
Cigna Medical Necessity Review
quick_train.clinical_context: The member is a 49-year-old woman with recurrent, treatment-resistant major depressive disorder dating to early 2024. She has documented inadequate response to five adequate antidepressant trials - sertraline 200 mg, venlafaxine XR 225 mg, bupropion XL 300 mg, escitalopram 20 mg, and aripiprazole augmentation - each at therapeutic dose for eight weeks or longer, alongside a course of cognitive behavioral therapy. Her two most recent PHQ-9 scores were 21 and 19. Her treating psychiatrist referred her for repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), which is FDA-cleared and recommended in the American Psychiatric Association major-depression practice guideline specifically for treatment-resistant depression after multiple failed pharmacologic trials, directly contradicting the denial's not-evidence-based characterization. The submitted records document each medication, dose, duration, and outcome, none of which the denial letter acknowledges. The fail-first step-therapy requirement the insurer imposes on this behavioral health service has no analogous requirement for comparable medical or surgical procedures under the same plan, which the treating provider has flagged as a parity concern under mental health parity rules.

quick_holdout.headline: case_126_cigna_mednec
quick_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter concerns the prior authorization request your treating provider submitted for gastric electrical stimulation (Enterra Therapy) implantation evaluation to treat your diabetic gastroparesis. After review under Cigna Medical Coverage Policy CPG 0178, the request is denied as not medically necessary. Determination date: May 28, 2026.

Cigna considers gastric electrical stimulation to be investigational and not evidence-based for gastroparesis. Our Medical Coverage Policy finds that the published peer-reviewed literature does not include randomized controlled trial evidence sufficient to show that the device improves net health outcomes for this indication. The coverage criteria applied here reflect efficacy data drawn predominantly from a geriatric study population (patients ages 65 and older), and the submitted records do not meet the threshold requirements in that criteria set. The requested procedure therefore does not satisfy our standard for medical necessity.

This decision was made by a Cigna medical reviewer using the information in the claim file. You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific medical necessity criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision.

If your provider would like to discuss this determination, a peer-to-peer review with a Cigna medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, together with any additional clinical information you would like considered. Appeals may be sent to the address shown on your Explanation of Benefits.

Sincerely,
Cigna Coverage Review Department
quick_holdout.clinical_context: The member is a 32-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.4%) and a two-year history of diabetic gastroparesis. A 4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy in February 2026 showed 38% retention at four hours, consistent with moderately severe delayed emptying. He has failed first-line prokinetic therapy: metoclopramide 10 mg four times daily was trialed for more than twelve weeks with persistent nausea, postprandial vomiting, and inability to maintain oral intake, and was ultimately limited by tremor and akathisia. Dietary modification to small, low-fat, low-fiber meals and a separate trial of erythromycin also failed to control symptoms. Over the preceding six months he lost approximately 7% of his body weight and required two emergency department visits for dehydration. His treating gastroenterologist documents that gastric electrical stimulation (Enterra Therapy) is indicated for refractory diabetic gastroparesis under the device's FDA Humanitarian Device Exemption, and that current peer-reviewed evidence supports its use in working-age adults whose disease is refractory to prokinetic agents. This is a young adult with treatment-refractory disease, not a patient from the geriatric cohort underlying the insurer's cited criteria.

serious_holdout.headline: case_109_aetna_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Aetna has completed its medical necessity review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider and received at our office on May 18, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated May 20, 2026.

After review against our utilization management criteria, we are unable to approve the requested service. Our cardiac device coverage criteria require documentation of ischemic cardiomyopathy with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35 percent or less before an implantable defibrillator is considered medically necessary. The information available does not establish that this threshold is met, and the requested service therefore does not satisfy the applicable criteria in effect.

This determination was made under our standard medical necessity criteria for cardiac devices. No review of the member's specific diagnosis or the individual clinical records submitted with the request was performed; the determination rests solely on the coverage criterion described above.

If you disagree with this decision, you may request an appeal by contacting Member Services at the number on your ID card within the time permitted under your plan.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Medical Review

Additional plan language regarding experimental/investigational definitions, network status, and member cost-sharing may apply and is available upon request.
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 22-year-old man with Brugada syndrome confirmed by a spontaneous type 1 Brugada pattern on resting ECG. He was referred after an episode of arrhythmic syncope without prodrome while at rest, and ambulatory monitoring captured a brief run of polymorphic ventricular ectopy. His father died suddenly at age 38, and genetic testing identified a pathogenic SCN5A variant. A transthoracic echocardiogram showed a structurally normal heart with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 62 percent, and coronary anatomy is normal for age, excluding ischemic cardiomyopathy. His electrophysiologist recommended an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator for prevention of sudden cardiac death, citing Heart Rhythm Society expert-consensus criteria, under which a spontaneous type 1 pattern with arrhythmic syncope and a family history of sudden death is a high-risk indication independent of ejection fraction. The prior authorization submission documented the ECG, the syncopal event, the family history, the SCN5A result, and the normal echocardiogram. Ejection-fraction-based criteria developed for ischemic cardiomyopathy do not apply to this channelopathy, in which sudden-death risk is arrhythmic rather than related to ventricular systolic function.

serious_holdout.headline: case_106_uhc_priorauth
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

UnitedHealthcare has reviewed the prior authorization request submitted on your behalf by the requesting facility and received at our office on March 12, 2026. This notice constitutes our coverage determination dated March 13, 2026.

After review by an Optum behavioral health reviewer, we are unable to authorize the requested service. A required peer-to-peer review was not completed within the timeframe allowed under our utilization management process, and authorization cannot be issued where that step has not been completed. Accordingly, the request is denied.

This determination was made in accordance with UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines and the applicable Coverage Determination Guideline (CDG), using the standard criteria applied to requests of this type. Based on the information available, the documentation does not support authorization at this time. No individualized clinical review of the submitted records was conducted, as the procedural requirement above was not met.

You or the requesting facility may request a copy of the claim file and the specific clinical criteria applied to this determination at no charge by contacting the number on your member ID card. A peer-to-peer discussion may be arranged through Provider Services in connection with a future submission if requested.

If you disagree with this decision, you have the right to file an internal appeal within 180 days of the date of this letter. Appeals may be submitted in writing to UnitedHealthcare Appeals, P.O. Box 740816, Atlanta, GA 30374, or by calling the number on your member ID card. You may submit additional information for our review with your appeal.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
UnitedHealthcare Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 58-year-old man with severe alcohol use disorder (F10.20) and a history of complicated withdrawal. He presented to the emergency department with a CIWA-Ar score peaking at 17, accompanied by tremor, diaphoresis, and tachycardia (heart rate 112). The evaluating emergency physician documented that outpatient detoxification would be unsafe given his withdrawal severity, a prior alcohol-withdrawal seizure, and the absence of a reliable support person at home. Inpatient medical detoxification (3–5 days) was requested to permit a monitored benzodiazepine taper and cardiac monitoring. The ordering psychiatrist submitted the clinical record, including serial vital signs, the CIWA-Ar trend, an elevated GGT, and a blood alcohol level of 240 mg/dL on arrival. The treating team attempted to arrange the peer-to-peer review but documented that United did not return the scheduling call within the offered window. The submitted documentation establishes that the requested level of care meets ASAM criteria for medically managed inpatient withdrawal management.
